content.text:
*"A false balance is an abomination to the Lord, but a 
just weight is His delight."*{Proverbs 11:1}{Logic is the anatomy of thought.}{John Locke}

Two propositions that always have the same truth value are said to be 
**logically equivalent**. This concept is fundamental to simplifying 
logical expressions and constructing valid arguments.

content.title: Logical Equivalence
content.content:
Two compound propositions $P$ and $Q$ are **logically equivalent**, 
written $P \equiv Q$, if they have the same truth value for every possible 
assignment of truth values to their propositional variables.

content.text:
Logical equivalence means that $P$ and $Q$ are interchangeable in any 
logical expression. We can verify equivalence by showing that $P \liff Q$ 
is a tautology, or by comparing truth tables.

The most important equivalences are De Morgan's Laws, which relate 
negation to conjunction and disjunction.

content.title: De Morgan's Laws
content.content:
For any propositions $p$ and $q$:
$$
\lnot(p \land q) &\equiv \lnot p \lor \lnot q \\
\lnot(p \lor q) &\equiv \lnot p \land \lnot q 
$$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We verify eq:demorgan1 by truth table:

| $p$ | $q$ | $p \land q$ | $\lnot(p \land q)$ | $\lnot p$ | $\lnot q$ | $\lnot p \lor \lnot q$ |
| $\T$ | $\T$ | $\T$ | $\F$ | $\F$ | $\F$ | $\F$ |
| $\T$ | $\F$ | $\F$ | $\T$ | $\F$ | $\T$ | $\T$ |
| $\F$ | $\T$ | $\F$ | $\T$ | $\T$ | $\F$ | $\T$ |
| $\F$ | $\F$ | $\F$ | $\T$ | $\T$ | $\T$ | $\T$ |

The columns for $\lnot(p \land q)$ and $\lnot p \lor \lnot q$ are identical. 
The proof of eq:demorgan2 is similar.

content.title: Augustus De Morgan (1806--1871)
content.content:
De Morgan was a British mathematician and logician who helped establish 
symbolic logic as a rigorous discipline. A devout Christian, De Morgan 
saw no conflict between faith and logical inquiry. He once wrote that 
mathematics "has no need of the supernatural" yet operates on principles 
that point beyond the material world.

content.title: Fundamental Equivalences
content.content:
Let $p$, $q$, and $r$ be propositions. The following equivalences hold:

**Double Negation:**
\[ \lnot(\lnot p) \equiv p \]

**Commutative Laws:**
$$
p \land q &\equiv q \land p & p \lor q &\equiv q \lor p
$$

**Associative Laws:**
$$
(p \land q) \land r &\equiv p \land (q \land r) & 
(p \lor q) \lor r &\equiv p \lor (q \lor r)
$$

**Distributive Laws:**
$$
p \land (q \lor r) &\equiv (p \land q) \lor (p \land r) \\
p \lor (q \land r) &\equiv (p \lor q) \land (p \lor r)
$$

**Identity Laws:**
$$
p \land \T &\equiv p & p \lor \F &\equiv p
$$

**Domination Laws:**
$$
p \land \F &\equiv \F & p \lor \T &\equiv \T
$$

**Idempotent Laws:**
$$
p \land p &\equiv p & p \lor p &\equiv p
$$

**Absorption Laws:**
$$
p \land (p \lor q) &\equiv p & p \lor (p \land q) &\equiv p
$$
content.label: Theorem

content.text: The conditional can be expressed using other connectives:

content.title: Conditional Equivalences
content.content:
$$
p \limplies q &\equiv \lnot p \lor q \\
p \limplies q &\equiv \lnot q \limplies \lnot p \quad\text{(contrapositive)} 
$$
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The equivalence eq:contrapositive is especially important: a 
conditional is equivalent to its **contrapositive**. 
This fact underlies the proof technique of contraposition.

content.title: Converse and Inverse Are Not Equivalent
content.content:
Given $p \limplies q$:

- The **converse** is $q \limplies p$ (not equivalent)

- The **inverse** is $\lnot p \limplies \lnot q$ (not equivalent)

- The **contrapositive** is $\lnot q \limplies \lnot p$ (equivalent!)
A common logical error is assuming the converse is true when the original 
conditional is true.
